title: Audio: binary search on the answer — when the answer IS the search space

cues.label: The family
cues.headline: The answer IS the search space
cues.narration: A whole family of problems where you search a range of candidate answers for the boundary between infeasible and feasible.

cues.label: Koko
cues.headline: Minimize speed, greedy hours
cues.narration: Koko Eating Bananas: search on speed, sum ceiling divisions, check against h hours.

cues.label: Monotonicity
cues.headline: Faster is always enough
cues.narration: If speed k works, speed k+1 works too, because eating faster never takes more hours. The feasibility function is monotonic.

cues.label: Template
cues.headline: Range, feasibility, search
cues.narration: Three pieces: define the range, write the feasibility oracle, binary-search. Same code structure every time.

cues.label: Upper search
cues.headline: Maximize the minimum gap
cues.narration: Magnetic Force flips the direction: small gaps are feasible, large gaps are not. Upper search with round-up mid avoids the infinite loop.

cues.label: Split Array
cues.headline: Minimize max subarray sum
cues.narration: Same template: candidate is the max allowed sum, feasibility is a greedy scan counting subarrays, lower search for the leftmost feasible.

cues.label: Off-by-one
cues.headline: Round up prevents infinite loop
cues.narration: In upper search, rounding down mid when lo and hi differ by one causes an infinite loop. Round up: mid = (lo + hi + 1) / 2.

cues.label: Recognition
cues.headline: Monotonic feasibility implies BS-on-answer
cues.narration: The recognition rule: if you can write a monotonic can(candidate) in polynomial time, you have a binary search on the answer.
